### Post by simeon87 on 2011-06-05
This is not a language, it's a binary file, possibly compiled code.

---

### Post by r-senior on 2011-06-05
That's a compiled binary, not source code. Nobody expects you to read that. ;)

If you look at the manual page for 'rm', there's a link to here:

[http://www.gnu.org/software/coreutils/](http://www.gnu.org/software/coreutils/)

Buried in an archive file, you'll find the C source code for rm. Most similar command line utilities are written in C and a lot of them are in that archive.

You should be able to download GNU coreutils from the Ubuntu repositories too. If you enable the source code repositories and find it in Synaptic.

---

### Post by wmcbrine on 2011-06-05
The common term for this is "machine code". It is the lowest level of code (not counting micro-code, but let's not go into that) -- the processor's native language. All other code is ultimately translated into this form, either at compile time, or at runtime.

As r-senior says, the best way to understand it is to go to the actual human-readable source code from which it was compiled. However, yes, it *is* also possible to learn to read the native code.

First, you won't get very far with "cat" or "less". The minimum tool for this job is a hex editor, which would show you the file as a sequence of hexadecimal numbers. But much better would be what's called a "disassembler", which takes the machine code and translates it to a (barely) human-readable sequence. (Assemblers do the reverse -- they translate from this low-level readable representation, known as "assembly language", to machine code.)

---
